relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Abimelech goes to his mother's kin in Shechem to build a throne through bloodline appeal.
He frames seventy brothers as a threat and himself as bone and flesh.
Kinship bends Shechem's heart toward him.
Temple silver hires empty and reckless men.
Abimelech slaughters his brothers on one stone, but Jotham hides and lives.
Shechem makes the bramble king beneath the oak.
Jotham climbs Gerizim and demands that Shechem listen.
The trees go out to anoint a king.
The olive refuses to abandon oil that honors Divine and human.
The trees turn to the fig.
The fig refuses to abandon sweetness and fruit.
The trees turn to the vine.
The vine refuses to abandon wine that gladdens.
At last the trees ask the bramble.
The bramble offers impossible shade and threatens consuming fire.
Jotham asks whether Shechem acted with faithfulness.
He remembers Gideon risking his life for the people.
Shechem answered that house by killing seventy sons and crowning one brother.
If this was faithful, let them rejoice in the king they made.
If not, let fire pass both ways between Abimelech and Shechem.
Jotham escapes the brother who killed the others.
Abimelech rules three years.
A destructive spirit opens treachery between king and city.
The blood of seventy brothers begins returning to those who shed and funded it.
Shechem's lords become mountain ambushers and road robbers.
Gaal arrives and becomes another focus of trust.
Harvest celebration turns into cursing Abimelech in the god-house.
Gaal mocks Abimelech and calls for older Shechemite loyalty.
He boasts that he would remove Abimelech if the people were in his hand.
Zebul hears and burns with anger.
He sends warning to Abimelech in secret.
Night ambush is planned against the city.
Morning will expose the boast.
Four companies wait in darkness.
Gaal stands at the gate while ambush rises.
Zebul gaslights the first sighting as mountain shadows.
Gaal sees more clearly: companies are coming from the land's navel and the oak.
Zebul throws Gaal's own words back at him.
Gaal goes out to fight.
Abimelech drives him back, leaving bodies near the gate.
Gaal is expelled from Shechem.
The next day the people go into the field.
Abimelech sets another ambush and strikes them.
One force seizes the gate while others attack those in the field.
Shechem is killed, torn down, and salted.
The tower lords flee into the stronghold of El-berith.
Their hiding place is reported.
Abimelech teaches everyone to carry branches for burning.
The tower burns over about a thousand men and women.
Abimelech carries the fire-pattern to Thebez.
The city's people gather in a tower and climb to its roof.
Abimelech approaches to burn this tower too.
A woman drops an upper millstone and crushes his skull.
He asks to be killed by a man so a woman's hand will not define his memory.
When Abimelech dies, the army dissolves.
The evil he did to his father by killing brothers returns upon him.
Shechem's evil also returns, and Jotham's curse arrives.

contemplative_note: Judges 9 is a whole dissertation on bad power. The fruitful trees refuse to rule; the bramble accepts and burns everyone. Abimelech is what happens when insecurity, kinship politics, temple money, mercenaries, and misogynistic honor become government.
